coredata.dc:title: Carbamazepine removal from water by carbon dot-modified magnetic carbon nanotubes
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Yanchun
Ok, Yong Sik
Mohan, Dinesh
Pittman, Charles U.
Dou, Xiaomin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dot- and magnetite-modified magnetic carbon nanotubes (CMNTs) were synthesized and evaluated for carbamazepine removal from water. The adsorbent was characterized by multiple modern surface and microstructure analyzing techniques. CMNTs were composed of three components including carbon dots (CDs), carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and magnetite. CDs and CNTs introduce abundant carboxyl groups onto CMNTs and magnetite allows rapid magnetic separation of the adsorbent realizable after batch adsorption. This adsorbent has a moderately high adsorption capacity of 65 mg-carbamazepine/g-adsorbent at pH 7.0 ± 0.2, which is superior to many reported adsorbents. Carbamazepine was uptaken well in a wide pH range, regardless of the surface charging of CMNTs. Its adsorption on CMNTs was quite fast and reached 80% of removal during the initial 3 h. The mass transfer within CMNTs and the time-dependent utilization, exhaustion and depletion of the adsorption capacity were successfully described using a simplified homogeneous surface diffusion model (HSDM). The surface diffusion coefficients (D s) rose with increasing initial carbamazepine concentrations. After six regeneration and recycle experiments, the capacity loss of CMNTs was less than 2.2% at the conditions tested. FTIR spectra showed the characteristics of the components. Raman spectra implied a π-π electron donor-acceptor (EDA) interaction during adsorption. This work proposed a method of combining π-bond-rich materials (CNTs and CDs) and magnetite to make separable composite adsorbents with high affinity interactions between carbamazepine and carbon materials. The prepared adsorbent is attractive for carbamazepine removal due to its good performance, moderate cost, ease of separation, and ability to regenerate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adsorption
Carbon dots
Modified carbon nanotubes
Carbamazepine
Intraparticle diffusion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of pH on carbamazepine removal
Kinetics of carbamazepine uptake by CMNTs
Characterization the adsorbent before and after carbamazepine adsorption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image of carbon dot magnetic carbon nanotube (CMNT) after adsorption (100 nm).
XRD patterns of magnetic carbon nanotube (MNT) and the carbon dot magnetic carbon nanotube (CMNT).
Magnetization curve of the carbon dot magnetic carbon nanotube (CMNT) sample and a demonstration of magnetic separation.

Adsorption isotherm of carbamazepine on carbon dot magnetic carbon nanotubes at equilibrium. Experiments were conducted at 3–30 mg/L initial carbamazepine concentrations and an adsorbent loading of 0.2 g/L. Experiments with carbamazepine were conducted at pH 7 ± 0.2. Simulation of carbamazepine adsorption employed the Redlich-Peterson model.

Carbamazepine adsorption kinetics onto carbon dot magnetic carbon nanotube adsorbents (CMNTs). Initial carbamazepine concentrations 5, 10, 20, 30 mg/L; adsorbent dose 0.2 g/L; total solution volume, 500 mL; pH, 7.0 ± 0.2; temperature, 25 °C, and shaking time, 24 h. Simulations used (a) the pseudo-second-order model, (b) the double-exponential model (DEM) and (c) the homogeneous surface diffusion model (HSDM).

Intraparticle two-dimensional profile of the carbamazepine concentration through the center of a single particle at various times using initial carbamazepine concentrations of 5, 10, 20, 30 mg/L; adsorbent dose, 0.2 g/L; pH, 7.0 ± 0.2; shaking time, 24 h; temperature, 25 °C.

Evolution of the dimensionless radial carbamazepine concentration profiles, C p versus r inside the particle calculated from the HSDM batch adsorption model at initial carbamazepine concentrations of 5 mg/L, 10 mg/L, 20 mg/L and 30 mg/L; adsorbent dose, 0.2 g/L; pH, 7.0 ± 0.2; shaking time, 24 h; temperature, 25 ± 1 °C.

Re-adsorption ability of the carbon dot magnetic carbon nanotube after magnetic separation followed by regeneration via acetonitrile stripping. Initial carbamazepine concentration, 10 mg/L; adsorbent dose, 0.2 g/L; shaking time, 24 h; pH, 7.0 ± 0.2, and temperature, 25 °C. The used adsorbent was regenerated in acetonitrile solution for 1 h which was repeated as in each desorption procedure. (The dashed line corresponds to the capacity of the fresh adsorbent).
(a) FTIR and (b) Raman spectra of CMNT before and after adsorption of carbamazepine from a 30 mg/L carbamazepine aqueous solution.
A performance comparison between the CMNT adsorbent and other adsorbents for removing carbamazepine from water.

row.entry: Silica-based material and powdered activated carbon

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: PC = Adsorption capacity/Final concentration = Adsorption capacity/(Initial concentration * (1-removal rate)).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fitting parameters of the homogeneous surface diffusion model (HSDM) for carbamazepine uptake by CMNT.

head.ce:title: Carbamazepine removal from water by carbon dot-modified magnetic carbon nanotubes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing 100083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Biochar Research Center, O-Jeong Eco-Resilience Institute (OJERI) & Division of Environmental Science and Ecological Engineering, Korea University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Sciences, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi 110067, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Sciences, Jawaharlal Nehru University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University, Mississippi State, MS 39762, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University, Mississippi State

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dot- and magnetite-modified magnetic carbon nanotubes (CMNTs) were synthesized and evaluated for carbamazepine removal from water. The adsorbent was characterized by multiple modern surface and microstructure analyzing techniques. CMNTs were composed of three components including carbon dots (CDs), carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and magnetite. CDs and CNTs introduce abundant carboxyl groups onto CMNTs and magnetite allows rapid magnetic separation of the adsorbent realizable after batch adsorption. This adsorbent has a moderately high adsorption capacity of 65 mg-carbamazepine/g-adsorbent at pH 7.0 ± 0.2, which is superior to many reported adsorbents. Carbamazepine was uptaken well in a wide pH range, regardless of the surface charging of CMNTs. Its adsorption on CMNTs was quite fast and reached 80% of removal during the initial 3 h. The mass transfer within CMNTs and the time-dependent utilization, exhaustion and depletion of the adsorption capacity were successfully described using a simplified homogeneous surface diffusion model (HSDM). The surface diffusion coefficients (D s) rose with increasing initial carbamazepine concentrations. After six regeneration and recycle experiments, the capacity loss of CMNTs was less than 2.2% at the conditions tested. FTIR spectra showed the characteristics of the components. Raman spectra implied a π-π electron donor-acceptor (EDA) interaction during adsorption. This work proposed a method of combining π-bond-rich materials (CNTs and CDs) and magnetite to make separable composite adsorbents with high affinity interactions between carbamazepine and carbon materials. The prepared adsorbent is attractive for carbamazepine removal due to its good performance, moderate cost, ease of separation, and ability to regenerate.

ce:def-description.ce:para: concentration of carbamazepine at time t (mg/L)
initial concentration of carbamazepine in aqueous solution (mg/L)
concentration of carbamazepine in CMNT solid particles (mg/L)
concentration of carbamazepine recorded in the experiments (mg/L)
concentration of carbamazepine predicted with the simulation (mg/L)
average mass of carbamazepine adsorbed per unit mass of adsorbent (mg/g)
mass of carbamazepine adsorbed per unit mass of adsorbent (mg/g)
mass of carbamazepine adsorbed per unit mass of adsorbent at equilibrium (mg/g)
distance in radial direction of CMNT particle (cm)

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing production and consumption of pharmaceutical products inevitably results in the release of a wide variety of synthetic organic compounds into the environment. Carbamazepine (CBZ), commercially known as ‘Tegretol’, is used for treatment of mental disorders, epilepsy, depression and trigeminal neuralgia. Carbamazepine is widely prescribed with an estimated global consumption of more than 1000 tons per year (Meribout et al., 2016; Garcia-Espinoza et al., 2018; Gurung et al., 2018). Therefore, it is widely distributed and detected in water (Gurung et al., 2018).

Occurrence of carbamazepine in surface water and wastewater has often been reported in varied concentrations. They range from 1 ng/L to 3600 ng/L in surface water, hospital and treatment plant wastewater (Crago and Klaper, 2018; Exposito et al., 2018; Fiedler et al., 2018), and from 10 to 443 mg/L in pharmaceutical industry effluents (Gadipelly et al., 2014; Dwivedi et al., 2016). The general physiochemical parameters are provided in Table 1. Although publicly available information is insufficient for conclusive risk analysis of environmental levels of carbamazepine on humans, some toxic experiments did show the adverse effect of carbamazepine on model microbe, flora and fauna species like bacteria, algae and medaka fish (Mohapatra et al., 2014). Carbamazepine discharge and occurrence in aquatic environment is a definite risk, especially at high exposure concentrations in the ppm level (Mohapatra et al., 2014). Thus, the effective treatment and mitigation of carbamazepine from contaminated water sources is urgent, and this issue has raised wide concerns in the academic community and pharmaceutical industry (Wang et al., 2018).

Several technologies have been evaluated for removing pharmaceutical compounds (e.g. carbamazepine) from water including microbial degradation, adsorption, photocatalysis, electrolysis, and membrane separation (Rivera-Utrilla et al., 2013; Gadipelly et al., 2014; Mohapatra et al., 2014; Wang and Wang, 2016; Gao et al., 2017). Complex hybrid technologies like a combination of advanced oxidation plus biological treatments were recommended for pharmaceutical remediation, including carbamazepine removal from wastewater (Gadipelly et al., 2014). Adsorption, with its simple design, low-cost, high efficiency and easy operation, is considered promising for mitigating pharmaceuticals (e.g. carbamazepine) from water (Rivera-Utrilla et al., 2013; Gadipelly et al., 2014; Mohapatra et al., 2014; Wang and Wang, 2016). Innovative adsorbents and their chemical behavior and uptake mechanisms have also been intensively studied for application in fix-bed column treatments (Carmalin Sophia et al., 2016; Ahmed and Hameed, 2018). Three advantages make adsorption attractive and competitive. First, it does not generate toxic or pharmaceutically active by-products during treatment (Rivera-Utrilla et al., 2013). Second, the kinetics can be accelerated by selecting adsorbents with fine-tuned heterogeneous pores, in which pharmaceutical mass transfer is rapid and adsorption is fast (Rivera-Utrilla et al., 2013). Third, adsorption units can be significantly enhanced by employing high performance novel adsorbents.

Low cost activated carbons are traditionally used for pharmaceuticals removal (Rivera-Utrilla et al., 2013). However, their overall adsorption rates and uptake abilities should be improved. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have excellent capabilities for removing emerging pharmaceutical contaminants including carbamazepine (Oleszczuk et al., 2009). High surface area is a primary property contributing to high organic compound adsorption capacity of CNTs (Lowry et al., 2010). Additional factors affect CNT sorption of organic pollutants, including sorbent and sorbate properties, solution chemistry (e.g. pH and ionic strength) and the presence of exogenous organic matter (Lerman et al., 2013). Carbamazepine adsorption on multiwalled CNTs has been intensively investigated with a focus on solution chemistry and competition of co-existing pharmaceuticals or dissolved organic matter (DOM) (Oleszczuk et al., 2009; Lerman et al., 2013). Complex mechanisms prevent general interpretations since differences of adsorbent surface activity, hydrophobicity, strength of π-π interactions and strength of electrostatic interactions, etc. are involved (Li et al., 2017).

Recent efforts were devoted to CNT functionalization to achieve better carbamazepine removal. CNT/alumina granular hybrid adsorbents were fabricated to adsorb carbamazepine from water. The removed adsorbate was decomposed at 400 °C regenerating the CNT/alumina adsorbent (Wei et al., 2013). Multiwall CNTs and other carbon materials decorated with magnetic substances (e.g. magnetite, FeCo or NiFe2O4 nanoparticles) have been evaluated for enhanced sorption of organic contaminants (Wu et al., 2012; Shan et al., 2016; Alizadeh Fard and Brian, 2018), followed by easy magnetic separation. New carbon-based adsorbents for organic pollutants removal are continuously being evaluated. Carbon dots with abundant surface carboxyl and hydroxyl groups were employed to make new hybrid adsorbents with good performances (Zhang et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2015b). In the present study, a hybrid adsorbent (CMNTs) was prepared by modifying multiwall carbon nanotubes with magnetite and carbon dots to mitigate and rapidly separate carbamazepine.

The main objectives were (1) to evaluate the effect of operation conditions on carbamazepine removal by CMNTs, (2) to investigate the adsorption kinetics and equilibrium of carbamazepine within CMNT particles and the regeneration of this adsorbent, and (3) to study the carbamazepine/CMNTs interaction using multiple techniques, including FTIR, Raman and Boehm titration.

All chemicals were analytical grade reagents. The carbamazepine solution was prepared with carbamazepine powder (J&K Chemical Co. Ltd.). The reported solubility of carbamazepine in water varied from 112 to 236 mg/L at room temperature (Shayanfar et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2013). There was no consistent and definite value reached. However, the solubility of carbamazepine in methanol is high, reaching at 28.5 °C (Liu et al., 2008). Dissolving carbamazepine in methanol and then diluting in water is the general procedure for preparing carbamazepine stock solutions (Zhao et al., 2016; Jakubus et al., 2017). Thus, the stock solution of carbamazepine was freshly prepared by dissolving 0.1000 g of carbamazepine powder in 20.00 mL methanol and then diluting the solution to 1000 mL using deionized water. The stock solution concentration is 100 mg/L.

Carbon dot-modified magnetic carbon nanotubes (CMNTs) were prepared by three consecutive steps including (1) the preparation of magnetic carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), (2) the synthesis of carbon dots and (3) the surface modification of magnetic multiwalled carbon nanotubes using carbon dots.

Magnetic carbon nanotubes (MNTs) were synthesized via a modified procedure by Li et al. (2009). Carbon nanotubes (2 g) were added into 112.5 mL of 0.1 mol/L NaOH solution and stirred for 1 h, and then 67.5 mL of 0.1 mol/L FeCl2 was slowly added into the solution with intense stirring for another 15 min. Further, 22.5 mL of 0.1 mol/L NaNO3 was slowly added and mixed for 10 min. The resultant solution was aged at 60 °C for 4 h and vacuum filtered. The separated solid was subsequently evaporated to dryness under ambient pressure over 24 h. After drying, the product was manually ground into powder for further use. The specific surface area of magnetic carbon nanotubes is 202.7 m2/g.

Carbon dots were prepared using a hydrothermal method (Chen et al., 2017). First, anhydrous citric acid (2.1 g) was mixed with 50 mL HNO3 (10%), 500 μL 1,2-diaminoethane and 50 mL deionized water in a Teflon-lined autoclave. The reactor was sealed and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling, the solution was neutralized using 1 mol/L of Na2CO3, filtered through 0.45 µm cellulose acetate membranes, and then dried using a rotary evaporator. The products were re-dispersed and washed twice with alcohol and finally vacuum dried at 45 °C for 2 h.

Magnetic carbon nanotubes (5 g) and carbon dots (0.1 g) were added into a 250 mL glass beaker with 100 mL deionized water. The suspension was shaken at 180 rpm at 25 °C for 12 h. Then the suspension was dried in a rotary evaporator until all water were evaporated. The collected solid was further oven dried at 60 °C for 2 h. The final product was ground manually into powder for further use. The particle size of the CMNT powders was measured using a Mastersizer 3000 laser particle size analyzer (Malvern Panalytical, United Kingdom). The average particle size of the CMNTs adsorbent is 74 µm as indicated in Fig. S1. The adsorbent has a true density of 1.051 g/cm3, which was measured by an AccuPyc II 1340 true density analyzer. The specific surface area of the carbon dot-modified magnetic carbon nanotubes (CMNTs) is 184.1 m2/g.

Surface morphologies of the samples were observed using a S-5500 field emission scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). XRD patterns of the samples were collected on an XRD-7000 (Shimadzu, Japan) using a Cu Kα radiation source. The diffractometer was operated at 40 kV and 40 mA and scanned at 5°/min from 10° to 80°. The specific surface area, pore volume and pore size distribution of samples were determined by a N2 adsorption-desorption method on an ASAP 2460 surface area and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics, USA). Magnetic properties of the sample were measured using a VSM 7307 vibrating sample magnetometer (Lakeshore, USA). The total surface acidity of CMNTs was determined using a revised method of Boehm titration by Goertzen et al. (2010). The deionized water used in Boehm titration was boiled and cooled to room temperature in a 2-L narrow-necked conical flask. The sample solutions were heated to 80 °C on a hot plate for 30 min and then sealed and left to cool to room temperature before titration. Thermogravimetry (TG) and differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) analyses were performed on a STA 449 F3 Jupiter® simultaneous thermal analyzer at a heating rate of 20 °C/min from 25 °C to 1000 °C with pure nitrogen as a purge gas at a flow rate of 40 mL/min (NETZSCH GmbH & Co, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded at room temperature on a Vertex 70 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, German) at a 2 cm−1 resolution. Raman spectra were obtained using a LabRAM HR800 Raman spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France) at the high-resolution mode with a 1.0 cm−1 resolution. The focal length was 800 mm. A noble-gas ion laser of 514 nm (Cvi Melles Griot, USA) at 25 mW was employed. The diffraction grating was 600 gr/mm. An air-cooled 1024 × 256 pixel full area CCD detector was installed and used.

Carbamazepine adsorption was carried out with varied initial concentrations (3–30 mg/L) and a fixed adsorbent dose (0.2 g/L) in 250 mL glass bottles. The total volumes were 100 mL. The suspensions were shaken at 150 rpm and at 25 ± 1 °C. Solution pH was maintained at 7.0 ± 0.2 through frequent manual adjustments (every 2 h) by using 1 mol/L HCl and NaOH. The suspension was sampled at preset intervals, filtered through a 0.22 µm cellulose acetate membrane, and then kept at 4 °C immediately. It was estimated that the adsorption results could be biased by 3–5% by using this organic polymeric membrane, since a little carbamazepine might be retained in the membrane. These samples were determined within 36 h on a high efficiency liquid chromatograph (HPLC) equipped with a UV detector (Shimadzu, Japan). The effect of pH on carbamazepine removal was studied in 250 mL glass bottles with a total volume of 100 mL. The initial carbamazepine concentration was 20 mg/L and the adsorbent dose was 0.2 g/L at 25 ± 1 °C. The other conditions were kept the same except for solution pH.

Effect of contact time on carbamazepine removal by CMNTs was evaluated at four initial concentrations ranged from 5 to 30 mg/L with a fixed adsorbent dose (0.2 g/L) in serials of 1000 mL glass bottles. The total volumes were 500 mL. Before withdrawing aliquots, each bottle was manually shaken intensively each time. Approximately 4 mL aliquots were taken at 0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 3.5, 4, 6, 8, 10, 24 h from the suspension by 5-mL plastic injectors without needles. Since the density of the CMNT particle (1.051 g/cm3) is very near to that of water (0.997 g/cm3 at 25 °C), the particle size is small (74 µm on average), and the suspension was well shaken, the well-distributed adsorbent powders and solution were drawn simultaneously when sampling each time. The solid/solution or the dosage of adsorbent did not change observably.

Regeneration and reuse were evaluated in a batch mode. First, CMNTs were put into 50 mL of carbamazepine solution with an initial concentration of 10 mg/L, to reach an adsorbent dose of 0.2 g/L at pH 7.0 ± 0.2. After shaking at 160 rpm for 24 h, CMNTs were recovered by magnetic separation. The desorption of carbamazepine was performed by stirring the used samples in 40 mL acetonitrile with a ratio of 20 mL-acetonitrile/0.1 g-adsorbent for 1 h at 25 ± 1 °C (Ghiasvand et al., 2015; Rivoira et al., 2017). This procedure was repeated twice. Then the adsorbent was washed with deionized water, dried at 60 °C for 12 h and reused in the next cycle up to 7 recycle procedures.

The adsorbent appears as aggregated spherical structures (ca. 10–20 nm) and fiber-like shapes in its SEM image ( Fig. 1). The spherical particles have uniform sizes (ca. 10–20 nm) and relatively smooth surfaces. They could be the aggregations of magnetite nanoparticles plus carbon dot nanoparticles. It is hard to identify the two kinds of nanoscale components from each other using only SEM images. The randomly interconnected networks of fibers are from multi-wall nanotubes (diameter ∼ 8 nm; length ∼ 10–30 µm). EDS elemental mapping (Fig. S2) clearly reveals the existence of elements Fe, O and C on the surface of CMNTs. The total acidities of magnetic carbon nanotubes (MNTs) and the carbon dot magnetic carbon nanotubes (CMNTs) determined by Boehm titration are presented in Table S1. The total acidity of CMNTs were almost two times of that of MNTs. The increase of acidity in CMNTs might be due to the loading of carbon dots into MNTs, since carbon dots were synthesized from citric acid as the starting material and the products were rich in acidic groups on surface. Abundant carboxyl groups (0.1 mg/mL) were reported on carbon dots’ surface (Kong et al., 2014). In addition, it needs to be clear that the Boehm titrations procedures are not designed for detecting the Fe-OH groups which are present on MNTs and CMNTs, so the results for Fe-OH are not available.

The CMNT’s XRD pattern ( Fig. 2) includes two sets of characteristic peaks. The medium broadened peak at 25.4° is from the overlapped diffractions of carbon nanotubes and carbon dots (Kawasaki et al., 2005; Wang et al., 2015a). The diffraction peak at 64.2° is assigned to Fe2O3 (Jiang and Gao, 2003; Ahmad et al., 2016) and the peak at 77.5° is assigned to carbon nanotubes (Cao et al., 2001). The other set of peaks at 18.12°, 30.06°, 42.88°, 53.32°, 56.98° and 62.44° are assigned to magnetite, Fe3O4 (PDF no. 19-0629). The magnetic hysteresis loop of CMNTs ( Fig. 3) displays a maximum saturated magnetization of 5.64 emu/g, which permits the quick separation of used adsorbent powders from solution. Powder adsorbents can better maintain their reactive absorption ability, compared to its granular forms. The former keep a smaller size, larger surface area and do not endure the granulation procedure and lose their reactivity partially.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of MNT and CMNTs are presented in Fig. S3. MNT has a BET surface area of 202.7 m2/g, while a modification by nanosized carbon dots reduced the surface area to 184.1 m2/g. The loss of surface area may be partially or mostly due to coating by Fe3O4 particles on MWCNTs. The pore sizes (hollow inside) of MNTs and CMNTs both range from 10 nm to 100 nm (Hu and Ruckenstein, 2004), which means the loading of carbon dots did not clog and change the pore size of the composites. The molecular size of carbamazepine is about 6.53 × 11.74 × 8.45 Å (Nielsen et al., 2014), which is much smaller than the pore size of the composite adsorbents. Thus, carbamazepine could diffuse into the pores, approach the inner-surface of pores and reach the active sites on the surfaces.

The TG and DSC curves of CMNTs giving ΔT rate and atmosphere (Fig. S4) were divided into three regions including (1) room temperature-100 °C; (2) 100–620 °C and (3) 620–1000 °C. The sharp and strong exothermic peak around 100 °C in the first region is attributed to the evolution of physically adsorbed water accompanied by a weight loss of 10%. The second broad and blunt region, occurring with large endotherm peaks ranged from 100 °C to 620 °C, corresponds to the thermal conversion of magnetite to maghemite or hematite, and the thermal decomposition of surface groups of carbon dots and CNTs. This is accompanied by a small weight loss of ca. 2%, which accounted for the intermolecular dehydration and carbonization of surface groups. Above 620 °C, a weight loss of 5% was observed, which was due to the partial thermal decomposition and combustion of the carbon nanotubes and carbon dots. The TG-DSC results reflected the thermal stability of this adsorbent and revealed that the drying and calcination temperature should be held to the early stage of the second transformation regions (less than 200 °C as stated in Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on carbamazepine removal
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S5 shows the effect of pH on carbamazepine removal by CMNTs. The carbamazepine uptake by CMNTs increases slightly as pH rises from 2.9 to 4.5, then remains steady from pH 4.5 to 11 with an average adsorption capacity averaged of 76 mg/g. That means the adsorbent maintains the ability to adsorb carbamazepine well over the entire range. The zeta potentials of CMNTs decreased continuously from pH 3.0 to 10.0 as shown in Fig. S6. The pHpzc of CMNTs is 5.3, while the pHpzc of magnetite and carbon nanotubes were reported to be 8.6–9.0 (Feng et al., 2016; Antal et al., 2018) and 6.0–8.2 (Elizabeth Roy et al., 2017; Gómez et al., 2017), respectively. Carbon dots pull down the overall pHpzc of the CMNT composite since the zeta potentials of carbon dots were negative over the pH range from 3 to 11 (Liu et al., 2017). In addition, the charge on CMNTs’ surface clearly changes over the pH range. The pKa of carbamazepine is 13.9, so carbamazepine is a neutral compound over the entire investigated pH range. It is striking that the negative and positive surfaces of CMNTs both can pick up this adsorbate. Thus, it is speculated the binding of carbamazepine onto CMNTs is not governed by electrostatic interactions.

Isotherm provides the basic information to compare and screen adsorbents and make decisions on which adsorbent should be selected and used. These parameters are basic for designing and operating adsorption modules. The carbamazepine adsorption equilibrium isotherms on CMNTs ( Fig. 4) were fitted using the Langmuir, Freundlich and Redlich-Peterson isotherm models. The results are summarized in Table S2. The Redlich-Peterson model fits the experimental data reasonably well (Fig. 4), yielding a determination coefficient (R
2 ) above 0.994. This suggests that carbamazepine uptake by CMNTs may not be restricted to either monolayer adsorption or definite multilayer adsorption. Complicated and heterogeneous processes might occur simultaneously on the variety of sites present, due to the intrinsic surface properties of carbon dots, carbon nanotube and magnetite. Also, the Redlich-Peterson is a three-parameter model, hence it has a fitting advantage on that basis. CMNTs have an adsorption capacity of 65 mg/g and a partition coefficient of 3.84 mg/(g·μM), which are larger than most of the adsorbents in Table 2, except for iron oxide nanoparticles when comparing adsorption capacities. The maximal adsorption capacity is sensitively affected by the initial concentration of target pollutant or more specifically by how much is left after sorption. If sorbent is exposed to higher concentration of pollutants, it should exhibit larger adsorption capacity and vice versa. Thus, it is a condition sensitive parameter. Hence, it is not good enough to use adsorbent capacity alone to meaningfully assess the actual performances of sorbents. Thus, an essential parameter, partition coefficient (PC), was employed along with adsorption capacity in each case. The partition coefficient represents the ratio of adsorbate concentration in and on adsorbent to its concentration in the liquid phase at equilibrium, suggesting that the partition coefficient can be used to simulate the true performance of an adsorbent (Vikrant and Kim, 2019). Thus, the partition coefficient (PC) was used further to compare the performances in Table 2. CMNTs have a larger PC value than that of iron oxide nanoparticles. It indicates that CMNTs are superior to the iron oxide nanoparticles, so they are good candidates for carbamazepine mitigation from water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetics of carbamazepine uptake by CMNTs
ce:section.ce:para:
Adsorption kinetic parameters are helpful in determining the minimum retention time of adsorbate in the units. In turn, they are basic when calculating the operational flow rate or specific surface velocity. Fig. 5(a) shows the carbamazepine uptake by CMNTs at four initial concentrations as time elapsed within 24 h. The adsorption of carbamazepine was very fast during the initial 3 h with more than 80% of carbamazepine removal achieved. Then a slow equilibrium process of 21 h follows. The kinetics datasets were fitted using the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order rate models (Lin and Wang, 2009) and the double-exponential model (DEM), which are expressed in Eqs. (1)–(3), respectively, in the form of C
t
/C
0 against time t. The parameter, q
e , is defined in Eq. (3).

t
)
+
D
2
exp
(
−
k
4
t
)
(4)
q
e
=
c
0
−
c
e
m
s

Here, q
e is the amount of carbamazepine adsorbed (mg/g solid material) at equilibrium; C 0, C
t and C
e are the concentrations of carbamazepine (mg/L) in solution at the initial stage, at equilibrium and at any time t, respectively; m s is the adsorbent dosage (g/L); k
1 (min−1), and k
2 (g/(mg min)) are the rate constants for the pseudo first- and second-order model, respectively. Both k 3 (h−1) and k 4 (h−1) are the mass-transfer coefficients, and D1 (mg/L) and D2 (mg/L) are the adsorption coefficients of the DEM model. t is the shaking time (min).

Fig. 5(a) and (b) present the fitting of the pseudo-second and DEM kinetics models. The calculated kinetic parameters are listed in Table S3. The large values of the determination coefficients (R 2 > 0.98) of the DEM model fits indicated that this kinetics model was preferred over the other two models. This model was employed to describe a two-stage kinetics with a rapid phase plus a slow phase as shown in Fig. 5 (Wilczak and Keinath, 1993; Shayan and Mirzayi, 2015). However, this model only generally and empirically described the concentration decline profile. The details of carbamazepine uptake and concentration evolution inside the solid adsorbent particles are needed to help understand the time-dependent utilization, exhaustion and depletion of the adsorption capacity inside CMNT particles. This knowledge will be helpful in optimizing the operational parameters during field applications.

Pollutant mass transfer inside porous materials has usually been described with three successive steps including external mass transfer (also called film diffusion), surface diffusion along the particle inner surface and adsorption on active surface sites. These processes generally account for the overall adsorption kinetics of porous adsorbents (Flora et al., 1998; Dou et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2016; Kwon et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018).

The external film barrier to mass transfer is negligible with CMNTs since the adsorbent was intensely shaken during adsorption (Yang et al., 2016). Meanwhile, the adsorption on the surface and internal active sites via chemical interactions occurs fast and is not a rate-limiting step. Under such circumstances, the model can be simplified to a Fick’s second law-controlled diffusion model, the homogeneous surface diffusion model (HSDM). In a 1-D spherical Cartesian coordinate system, the HSDM model is expressed in Eq. (5) (Zhang et al., 2009; Escudero et al., 2013).
(5)
∂
q
∂ t
=
1
r
2
∂
∂ r
(
D
s
r
2
∂ q
∂ r
)

The initial and boundary conditions are given as,
(6)
∂ q
∂ r
= 0 ,
r = 0
(7)
q = 0 ,
t = 0
(8)
q =
q
e
,
r =
R
p
,
t =
infinity

Here, q is the amount of carbamazepine adsorbed (mg-carbamazepine/g-solid material) at any time; r and R
p are the distance in radial direction of CMNT particle (cm) and the diameter of CMNT particle, respectively; D s is the intraparticle surface diffusion coefficient (cm2/min).

The average adsorbed concentration inside the adsorbent is,
(9)
q
¯
=
3
R
p
3
∫
0
R
p
q ( r , t )
r
2
d r =
(
C
0
− C
)
V
m
Where m is the mass of the adsorbent (g) and V is the volume of the carbamazepine solution (L).

Eq. (4) was solved numerically using the FlexPDE 7.0 Professional version. The best fits are obtained by minimizing an objective function, which finds the minimum value of the root mean-square error (RMSE) as expressed in Eq. (9) (Yang et al., 2016),
(10)
R M S E =
∑
i = 1
n
(
C
A ,
exp
−
C
A ,
mod
e l
)
2
n

The HSDM fittings are shown in Fig. 5(c) and Table 3. The low RMSE values indicated that the fittings are reasonable (Table 3). The mass transfer parameter (D s) increased from 2.71 × 10−18 to 4.01 × 10−13 cm2/min as the initial carbamazepine concentrations rose from 5 mg/L to 30 mg/L. This D s enlargement was possibly due to fast surface diffusion driven by the larger concentration gradient from the outer-surface to the spherical core.

After resolving the partial differential Eq. (5) and coupled Eq. (9) using FlexPDE, the adsorption capacity q inside the particle at any time t and at any radial position r was calculated. The radial position r was normalized to a 0–1 or −1 to 0 range by dividing the average radius of CMNT particle, Rp. The adsorption capacity q was normalized based on q e, the adsorption capacity at equilibrium. These results were plotted in gradient charts ( Fig. 6). These charts show the visual carbamazepine saturation (q/q e versus t) displays within CMNTs particles at six time increments for the four initial concentrations. The dynamics of adsorption capacity depletion is intuitively illustrated. Solid phase carbamazepine concentrations within CMNTs particles increased more rapidly for higher initial concentrations at each time increment. Accordingly, the concentration saturation from outer surface to inside cores along the radius developed much faster for higher initial concentrations. It took only 30 and 800 min, respectively, to get 75% and 90% saturation of the CMNTs particles at an initial 30 mg/L carbamazepine concentration, while 800 and 1500 min were spent for the same saturation status at a 5 mg/L initial concentration.

The carbamazepine concentration inside CMNT solid particles, Cp, at any time t and at any radial position r was calculated using the Freundlich isotherm model based on the fitted parameters (Table S2) and the corresponding q. The calculated Cp against time at any normalized radial position r were plotted and presented. Fig. 7 presents the evolution of carbamazepine concentration front profiles (C p versus t) inside CMNTs particles versus time. The carbamazepine diffusion dynamics can be compared for the four initial concentrations at any radius position from outer-surface to spherical center along the normal direction. Higher initial carbamazepine concentrations produced faster diffusion kinetics within the CMNTs particles. This was driven by the concentration-induced driving forces mentioned above.

The regeneration and recycle performance of CMNTs over 7 cycles is presented in Fig. 8. The reused CMNTs capacities remained unchanged for the first three cycles and dropped only slightly after the 6th cycle. In a practical treatment process, the influent concentration of carbamazepine is usually much lower than that was used to evaluate and obtain the maximum adsorption capacity. Thus, a better performance can be expected in practice. These results clearly illustrate that CMNTs are attractive for multiple reuse cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization the adsorbent before and after carbamazepine adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectra of CMNTs before and after carbamazepine adsorption are provided in Fig. 9(a). Little difference appeared before and after carbamazepine adsorption. The band around 3677 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching modes of -OH bands related to free water (surface adsorbed water) (Kong et al., 2017). The bands at 2985 cm−1 and 2900 cm−1 were due to the C-H stretching vibrations (Hafaiedh et al., 2012; Kafshgari et al., 2017). The band at 2352 cm−1 could be due to adsorbed O=C=O from air or some C≡C or R—N=C=O group (Ahmed et al., 2017). The band at 1068 cm−1 is due to the C-O stretching in -COOH groups of MWCNTs or carbon dots (Tucureanu et al., 2016). The bands at 528 cm−1 and 626 cm−1 were both assigned to Fe–O bond (Sathiyanarayanan et al., 2007; Namduri, 2008). The characteristic IR bands of carbamazepine were not observed, which usually appear at 1650 cm−1 for H-bonded C=O stretching, and at ~ 1300 cm−1 or 1320–1340 cm−1 for C-N stretching without or with H-bonding (Czernicki and Baranska, 2013). The missing of these characteristic IR bands was due to the low contents of adsorbed carbamazepine relative to the detection limit of the FTIR instrument. The adsorbate would only be present at the sample surface region or inner-pore surface. This made it detection by FTIR difficult.

Fig. 9(b) presents the Raman spectra of CMNTs before and after carbamazepine adsorption (30 mg/L). The bands at 1344 cm−1, 1582 cm−1 and 2695 cm−1 are assigned to the D band, G band and 2D band of carbon materials (e.g. carbon nanotube or carbon dots), respectively (Chen et al., 2009). The G peak is a result of in-plane vibrations of sp3 bonded carbon atoms whereas the D peak is due to out of plane vibrations attributed to the presence of structural defects (Zhang and Wang, 2014; Fontananova et al., 2017). The D/G ratio is relates to sp3/sp2 carbon ratio (Le et al., 2017). The ID/IG values were 1.45–1.90 for carbon nanotubes (Hidalgo-Manrique et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017) and 1.18–1.20 for carbon dots (Genc et al., 2017; Sarkar et al., 2017), respectively. CMNTs have an ID/IG value of 1.63, which was from the overall contributions of the nanotubes and carbon dots. After adsorption, both the G band and D band were blue shifted while the position of the 2D band was intact. The G-band is sensitive to an electron-donor-acceptor (EDA) interaction (Qin et al., 2017). In the present study, the G-band blue shift-indicated a π-π EDA interaction might occur. The π-electron-rich aromatic rings and heterocyclic ring of carbamazepine may interact with the π-electron-depleted regions on carbon dots or carbon nanotubes’ surfaces (Chen et al., 2008). In addition, H-binding could occur between carbonyl groups in CMNTs and the HN- function in carbamazepine. In short, π-π interaction and H-bonding can contribute to carbamazepine removal by the carbon materials making up the magnetic adsorbent.

A carbon dot-modified magnetic carbon nanotube (CMNT) adsorbent was fabricated by amending magnetic carbon nanotubes with carbon dots. This adsorbent has a good carbamazepine removal performance and is easily separated after adsorption. CMNTs exhibited a moderately high, 65 mg/g, adsorption capacity at pH 7.0 ± 0.2, which is higher than many reported adsorbents. Carbamazepine removal by CMNTs was rapid in the first 3 h and the kinetics were well fitted using the pseudo-second order and HSDM models. The simplified HSMD model can describe well the time-depended adsorption capacity depletion of CMNTs and carbamazepine diffusion occurring within the particles. The intraparticle diffusion parameter, D s increased with higher initial carbamazepine concentrations, due to the concentration-induced driving force within the particles. The effect of solution pH on carbamazepine adsorption suggested that carbamazepine binding was not governed by an electrostatic interaction. This adsorbent can be regenerated and reused up to 6 times with capacity loss less than 2.2%. FTIR spectra showed the characteristics of the MWCTN and carbon dot components. Raman spectra implied π-π interactions between the carbon components-based adsorbent and carbamazepine. H-bonding likely contributed to the adsorption also. In short, the two carbon components facilitate carbamazepine removal while the magnetic component made rapid separation feasible after use in batch processes. This adsorbent is attractive, effective, recyclable and its moderate cost and easy separation are suitable for carbamazepine remediation from water.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2017ZY41, 2015ZCQ-HJ-02).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.envres.2018.11.035.
